title: Danish pastry dough
subtitle: Laminated enriched dough for all Danish shapes
excerpt: The laminated enriched dough behind every Danish pastry: a yeasted détrempe folded around a butter block and turned three times to build 27 layers. The result is flakier and richer than croissant dough, less chewy, and sweetened through the dough itself. Use it for snails, twists, or filled pockets.
sourceNotes: Danish pastry (wienerbrød) was introduced to Denmark by Viennese bakers in the mid-nineteenth century. The original Viennese technique of laminating enriched dough with butter was adapted into a sweeter, more tender format by Danish bakers. The modern Danish pastry tradition is documented in Danish and Scandinavian baking literature from the late nineteenth century onward, and the technique spread internationally through Scandinavian immigrant communities, particularly in the United States.

content.text: Danish pastry lamination shares the same logic as croissant dough but starts from a richer, sweeter
must be at the same pliability as the dough when you begin folding, too cold and it shatters, too warm and it melts into the dough rather than layering. Three single
give 27 layers. Work quickly and keep everything cold.

The butter should flex without cracking when you press it with a rolling pin. If it shatters, return to room temperature for 5 minutes. If the dough becomes greasy or the butter starts to break through during rolling, stop, cover, and refrigerate for 20 minutes before continuing. You cannot rush lamination.

Combine the flour, sugar, salt, and yeast in a large bowl (or the bowl of a stand mixer). Add the milk and eggs and mix until a shaggy dough forms. Add the softened butter and mix until the dough comes together and is smooth but not fully developed, about 5 minutes by hand or 3 minutes with a dough hook on medium. Do not develop the gluten fully; the dough should look smooth but not springy. Shape into a flat rectangle, wrap, and refrigerate for 1 hour.

Place the butter between two sheets of baking paper and beat with a rolling pin to flatten into a rectangle approximately 15 × 20 cm. Fold the paper edges over to square off the sides. The butter should be pliable but not soft: it should flex without cracking. Refrigerate if needed.

On a lightly floured surface, roll the chilled détrempe into a rectangle roughly twice the size of the butter block. Place the butter in the centre and fold the dough over to enclose it completely, pressing the edges to seal. Roll out gently and evenly into a long rectangle three times longer than it is wide. Fold into thirds (letter fold), this is the first turn. Wrap and refrigerate for 30 minutes.

Repeat the rolling and letter fold twice more, resting the dough for 30 minutes between each turn. Mark the number of turns with finger indentations so you do not lose count. After the third turn, wrap and refrigerate for at least 1 hour before shaping.

Roll the rested dough to 4 mm and cut into the shapes required. For snails, cut rectangles and roll with a filling; for twists, cut strips and twist; for pockets, cut squares and fold over a filling with the corners tucked. Place on lined trays, brush with egg wash, and prove at room temperature for 1 to 2 hours until the pastries have puffed visibly and feel airy when prodded. Do not prove in a warm spot; a cool kitchen is better.

Preheat the oven to 200°C conventional (180°C fan). Brush proved pastries with a second coat of egg wash. Bake for 16 to 18 minutes until deep amber and the layers are visible and open at the sides. Cool on a rack for at least 15 minutes before eating, the interior is still steaming and collapsing hot.
